Before you touch Tailwind-heavy UI, I’d do this in order:

URL + controller map — Decide routes (e.g. /Workspaces, /Workspaces/Details/{id}, same pattern for Projects, Tasks, …) and add empty controllers with Index + Details(Guid id) that only resolve repos and return NotFound() when GetById is null. Lets you verify DI and IDs without markup.

404 behavior — Use consistent handling (either return NotFound() or a small shared helper) so every Details action behaves the same.

ViewModels (light) — For lists, use a small row DTO (title, key fields, id) so Razor doesn’t reach through huge graphs. Keep Details as the full entity or a dedicated details VM only if the view needs extra computed fields (breadcrumbs, parent name, etc.). You can start with @model Workspace and introduce VMs when views get noisy.

Custom home data — Sketch what the dashboard shows (reuse DemoDataLinqExamples or repo calls) and add a single HomeController action (or DashboardController) that builds one dashboard ViewModel. That’s the hardest “shape” decision; doing it before CSS saves rework.

Navigation model — Define how you’ll build breadcrumbs (e.g. Task → Board → Project → Workspace): either compute in the controller or a tiny view component. Decide once, reuse on Details pages.

Smoke checklist — After controllers exist, hit every Index/Details in the browser as plain HTML (minimal layout) to confirm links and Guids before you style.

Optional but useful: stabilize seed GUIDs in DemoDataBuilder so bookmarks and test URLs don’t change every run.

If you only do one thing: controllers + Details NotFound + dashboard ViewModel — then UI becomes mostly presentation. lets create a plan for this then
